design_philosophy.core_loop: Cursor-based block swapping to create matches of three or more blocks. Chain reactions trigger when blocks fall after clearing, rewarding spatial planning and combo execution. Rising block pressure creates escalating tension.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure from steadily rising blocks climbing toward top boundary. Player must clear blocks faster than they accumulate or lose.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Quick-thinking puzzle mastery through pattern recognition, spatial reasoning, and reactive chain-reaction engineering. Accessible skill-based competition.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Departs from traditional Tetris piece-falling paradigm entirely—uses grid-swapping mechanic instead. Emphasizes cursor control precision and immediate feedback loops over piece rotation planning. Design choice to simplify entry for casual players while enabling competitive depth through chain mechanics.

difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression in Story Mode; checkpoint at each opponent encounter
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Multiplayer modes feature adjustable difficulty levels. Multiple game mode options (Story, Endless, Timed, Stage Clear, Puzzle) allow players to self-select challenge type. Puzzle Mode preset arrangements can be solved at own pace.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Story Mode progresses through sequential opponent encounters. Mode unlocks tied to story completion.
replayability_hooks: Endless Mode for high-score chasing with increasing speed escalation
Puzzle Mode preset arrangements with move-limited solutions
Stage Clear mode requiring boundary-specific clearing
Multiplayer competitive modes with difficulty scaling
Character-specific story routes (original Japanese Panel de Pon featured fairy characters; North American Tetris Attack features Yoshi characters)

world_building_techniques: Character roster via opponent selection (Story Mode matches against series of characters)
Background art changes per stage/character in Endless Mode (e.g., Lakitu's background, character-specific environmental themes)
Thematic character pairing: Japanese version used fairy characters in mythical fantasy setting; North American version imported Yoshi universe IP overlay

faction_indicators: Character roster in Story Mode represents distinct opponents with implied personalities
Multiplayer modes enable head-to-head competitive play with scoring differentiation
Regional variance signals: Japanese Panel de Pon identity vs. North American Tetris Attack/Yoshi branding creates cultural faction split
danger_signals: Rising block visual stress indicator; proximity to top boundary communicates escalating threat
Speed acceleration in Endless Mode communicates mounting difficulty pressure
Opponent AI responsiveness in multiplayer creates competitive threat perception
environmental_storytelling: Background art rotation per character/stage establishes visual personality hierarchy
Cursor-based control mechanic emphasizes player agency and precision over passive observation
Game mode titles (Story, Endless, Stage Clear, Puzzle) create implicit narrative context through mechanical framing

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Tetris Attack SNES dev history, regional differences, unused assets)
wikipedia (Tetris Attack - gameplay mechanics, development credits, release dates, reception summary)
